nameMeanings.name: atte
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (northern):
from an unrecorded Middle English personal name Tate (Old English Tāta, from tāt ‘happy’).
usually a variant of   Tait  .

variants.name: Tates
meanings: English (Lancashire): variant of Tate , with post-medieval excrescent -s. This surname is now very rare in Britain. In the US, it is found mainly among African Americans.

variants.name: Tade
meanings: Possibly an altered form of English   Tate  .

North German and East Frisian: from a Frisian short form of a personal name composed with the ancient Germanic element theud ‘people, race’ (see Tack , compare Thaden ). This surname is very rare in Germany and is apparently not found in East Frisia.
Japanese (pronounced as two syllables): written 蓼 ‘knotweed’. Not a common surname.

variants.name: Tait
meanings: Scottish and northern English: nickname for a cheerful person, from Middle English and Older Scots tait(e) ‘merry, eager, lively’ (Old Norse teitr), also used as a personal name. Compare Tate .

variants.name: Tasso
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Carmine, Giancarlo, Gianpaolo, Lorenzo.  German Dieter.
Italian: topographic name for someone who lived near a prominent yew tree, Italian tasso (from Latin taxus, genitive taxi).
Italian: from the personal name Tas(s)o, derived from the obscure ancient Germanic element tāt, possibly akin to Old High German tāt ‘deed’. Compare English Tate .

nameMeanings.name: Mede
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Bartolo, Croce, Dante, Domenico, Giorgio, Luigi, Pietro, Salvatore, Vincenzo, Vito.  French/Haitian Guerline, Jean Marie.
Italian: from the female personal name Fede, from Latin fides ‘faith’.
Italian: from a short form of the personal name   Federico  .

variants.name: Federico
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Domenic, Antonio, Guerino, Armando, Salvatore, Domenico, Gino, Mario, Nino, Sal, Carmine, Guido, Alfredo, Angelo, Benito, Carmela.  Spanish Jose, Ruben, Carlos, Jesus, Rosario, Enrique, Francisco, Juan.
Italian: from the personal name Federico, of ancient Germanic origin (see Friedrich ). Compare Federick and Frederico .

variants.name: Di Fede
meanings: Italian (Sicily): patronymic or metronymic from the male or female personal name   Fede  .

nameMeanings.name: Nicholas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: form of the post-classical Greek personal name Nikolaos, derived from nikē ‘victory’ + laos ‘people’. The spelling with -ch- first occurred as early as the 12th century, and became firmly established at the time of the Reformation, although Nicolas is still occasionally found. St Nicholas was a 4th-century bishop of Myra in Lycia, about whom virtually nothing factual is known, although a vast body of legend grew up around him, and he became the patron saint of Greece and of Russia, as well as of children, sailors, merchants, and pawnbrokers. His feast day is 6 December , and among the many roles which legend has assigned to him is that of bringer of Christmas presents, in the guise of ‘Santa Claus’ (an alteration of the Dutch form of his name, Sinterklaas).

variants.name: Nickolas
meanings: Mainly U.S.: variant spelling of   Nicholas  , influenced by the short form   Nick .

variants.name: Nicolas
meanings: From Late Greek. See Nicholas .

historicEvents.title: Famine Strikes Europe
historicEvents.description: A famine caused by cold winters and wet summers across Europe caused millions to die due to starvation. Desperation from hunger caused high levels of crime, stampedes in bread lines in London, infanticide, and even cannibalism throughout Europe.

historicEvents.title: First War of Scottish Independence
historicEvents.description: The First War of Scottish Independence began after England invaded Scotland in 1296. The Treaty of Edinburgh was signed in 1328 which declared peace between the two kingdoms and brought the war to an end.

historicEvents.title: The Hundred Years' War
historicEvents.description: The Hundred Years' War between France and England was largely due to the question of legitimate succession to the French crown.

historicEvents.title: Wycliffe's Bible
historicEvents.description: John Wycliffe, English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, completed a translation of the Bible from the Latin Vulgate into Middle English. This translation is known as Wycliffe's Bible and was completed around 1382.

en.lifeSummary: Nicholas atte Mede was born about 1305, in Wraxall, Somerset, England as the son of Thomas Meade and Joyce Hart. He had at least 1 son with Mary Sharp. He died about 1397, in Taunton, Somerset, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 94.

fr.lifeSummary: Nicholas atte Mede est né environ 1305, à Wraxall, Somerset, Angleterre, fils de Thomas Meade et Joyce Hart. Il a eu au moins 1 fils avec Mary Sharp. Il est décédé environ 1397, à Taunton, Somerset, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 94 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Nicholas atte Mede wurde um 1305 in Wraxall, Somerset, England, als Sohn von Thomas Meade und Joyce Hart geboren. Er hatte mit Mary Sharp mindestens 1 Sohn. Er starb um 1397 im Alter von 94 Jahren in Taunton, Somerset, England, United Kingdom.

it.lifeSummary: Nicholas atte Mede è nato il circa 1305 nel Wraxall, Somerset, England come figlio di Thomas Meade e Joyce Hart. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio con Mary Sharp. Morì circa 1397, nel Taunton, Somerset, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, all'età di 94 anni.

ko.lifeSummary: Nicholas atte Mede(은)는 대략 1305년 영국 서머세트 라스알에서 Thomas Meade(와)과 Joyce Hart의 아들로 태어났다. 그는 Mary Sharp(와)과 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 대략 1397년 영국 잉글랜드 서머세트 타운턴에서 94세의 나이로 사망했다.
